coredata.dc:title: A pH visual sensing platform based on dual-emission chiral carbon dots for discrimination of normal/cancer cells and monitoring food freshness
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Mengyao
Huang, Yongfei
Fan, Zhefeng
coredata.dc:description: Accurate detection of pH is important in pathological processes and food freshness. Developing sensors of sensitive response and visualization for pH is highly demanded. In this work, Chiral carbon dots (CCDs) was synthesized via one-pot hydrothermal process using o-phenylenediamine and L-Tryptophan, which displayed circular dichroism (CD) signals at 200–255 nm and 255–300 nm. The CCDs exhibited dual-emission peaks with blue and red emission bands when excited at 360 nm. The ratiometric signals of UV–vis absorption and fluorescence intensity of L-CDs were responsive over the pH range of 2.0–11.0 with significant color changes in solution. Fluorescence imaging of live cells displayed different signals related to pH in both the blue and red channels, allowing accurate measurement of the pH of the cellular environment. Furthermore, the pH test paper based on L-CDs enabled monitoring the freshness of shrimp and pork under 365 nm UV light. Therefore, L-CDs provided a multifunctional visual pH sensing platform for environmental monitoring and biosensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: pH visual sensing
Dual-emission
Chiral carbon dots
Cell recognition
Meat freshness monitoring

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Monitoring the freshness of meat samples
Distinguishing normal cells and cancer cells
Paper-based sensor for pH detection in food samples

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (inset: the size distribution histogram); (b) HRTEM image; (c) XRD pattern of L-CDs; (d) FT-IR spectra of L-Trp/OPD/L-CDs.
(a) XPS spectrum, (b) high-resolution XPS of C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s of L-CDs.
(a) UV–vis spectra, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of L-CDs, (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of L-CDs, (c) L-CDs@SiO2 at different excitation wavelengths in B-R (pH = 7) solution.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of L-CDs in B-R (pH = 7) with different ethanol content. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of L-CDs at different excitation wavelengths, (c) TEM images of L-CDs, (d) CD spectrums of L/D-CDs in EtOH/B-R (1/1, v/v, pH = 7.0) solution.

(a)Fluorescence spectra and (d) UV–vis spectra of L-CDs in EtOH/B-R (1/1, v/v) (Insets: Picture of L-CDs at different solutions under sunlight and 365 nm UV lamp. (b) The ratiometric of fluorescence intensity (F608/F438) and (e) UV–vis absorption (A540/A630) of L-CDs in EtOH/B-R (1/1, v/v) solution. (c) Linear fitting of ratiometric FL intensity (F608/F438) with the pH of B-R increasing from 4.0 to 8.0; (f) Linear fitting of ratiometric UV–vis absorption (A540/A630) with the pH of B-R increasing from 5.0 to 9.0.

(a) Fluorescence reversible response against pH repeatedly varied between 4.0 and 8.0. (b) The time-dependent fluorescence intensity of L-CDs under Xe lamp irradiation. (c) Effect of ionic strength. (d) Fluorescence intensity in the presence of various species of L-CDs in EtOH/B-R (1/1, v/v) solution.
Fluorescence images of HepG2 and HL-7702 cells incubated with the L-CDs and quantitative analysis results.

(a) Method for preparing photo of pH test paper. (b) Photo of pH test paper moistened with different pH of B-R solutions. (c) Photo of meat (shrimp and pork) and pH test paper at different temperatures for various times under 365 nm UV light.
Preparation and Application in detection of pH of the L-CDs.
Schematic illustration of dual-emission mechanism of L-CDs.

head.ce:title: A pH visual sensing platform based on dual-emission chiral carbon dots for discrimination of normal/cancer cells and monitoring food freshness

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030031, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030031, People's Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Changzhi University, Changzhi 046011, People's Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Changzhi University, Changzhi 046011, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate detection of pH is important in pathological processes and food freshness. Developing sensors of sensitive response and visualization for pH is highly demanded. In this work, Chiral carbon dots (CCDs) was synthesized via one-pot hydrothermal process using o-phenylenediamine and L-Tryptophan, which displayed circular dichroism (CD) signals at 200–255 nm and 255–300 nm. The CCDs exhibited dual-emission peaks with blue and red emission bands when excited at 360 nm. The ratiometric signals of UV–vis absorption and fluorescence intensity of L-CDs were responsive over the pH range of 2.0–11.0 with significant color changes in solution. Fluorescence imaging of live cells displayed different signals related to pH in both the blue and red channels, allowing accurate measurement of the pH of the cellular environment. Furthermore, the pH test paper based on L-CDs enabled monitoring the freshness of shrimp and pork under 365 nm UV light. Therefore, L-CDs provided a multifunctional visual pH sensing platform for environmental monitoring and biosensing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The accurate detection of tiny changes in the pH value is of great importance for chemical reactions, environmental monitoring, biological activity, and drug delivery [1–4]. Moreover, the pH value of the cell environment is a significant tumor biomarker. Rather than metabolizing glucose to pyruvate as is typically in healthy cells, anaerobic glycolysis in cancer cells metabolizes glucose to lactic acid, leading to cancer cells becoming somewhat acidic [5]. The issue of food safety has become increasingly prominent in recent years [6]. Food safety is directly impacted by food freshness [7]. The alkaline gas biogenic amines (BAs) produced by the breakdown of proteins in spoiled meat by microbes and enzymes cause changes in the pH of food and packaging [8]. pH can be detected by using pH paper, electrochemistry methods, Raman spectroscopy, absorption spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy [9–13]. Among them, fluorescence spectroscopy has the advantages of visible detection, high sensitivity, minimal damage to samples, and real-time monitoring, which is considered an optimal choice [14]. Therefore, the development of fluorescent probes that can accurately detect subtle pH changes accompanied by visible color changes and be successfully used to monitor food freshness and cellular imaging remains a challenging problem.

Carbon dots (CDs), an innovative zero-dimensional nanomaterial smaller than 10 nm, have garnered significant interest for its simple synthesis process, low cost, superior photostability, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility [15]. Chirality is the ubiquitous feature of nature's world, encompassing scales from subatomic particles through molecules to galaxies [16]. Since the chirality has been observable at the nanoscale, research on the design and synthesis of optically active chiral carbon dots (CCDs) has been rapidly evolving [17,18]. Due to their intrinsic chiral signals, CCDs not only inherit the benefits of standard CDs but also play important roles in the fields of chiral recognition, biosensing, antibacterial, and regulation of cellular energy metabolism [19–24]. Nevertheless, most of the reported CCDs are single-emission [15]. As a result, the accuracy of the analytical results is highly susceptible to the fluctuation of excitation source, test microenvironment, concentration of the probe, and light scattering from the sample matrix, which greatly limits their application in bioimaging [25]. Ratiometric fluorescent probes are analyzed and detected according to the change in the intensity ratio of two emission peaks, which can improve the reliability and accuracy of detection [26]. There are three main routes to construct CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent probes: physical mixing, nanohybrid, and synthesis of dual-emission carbon dots in one-step [27]. Several disadvantages can not be ignored in the physical mixing and nanohybrid strategies, such as high preparation cost, complex synthesis steps, and poor stability [28]. However, to the best of our knowledge, CCDs with dual-emission in one-step have not been reported. Therefore, it is necessary to synthesize the dual-emission CCDs at single excitation in one-step.

Aiming at the above problems, CCDs with dual-emission via one-step hydrothermal method was synthesized. Two symmetrical circular dichroism (CD) signals of L-CDs displayed at 200–255 nm and 255–300 nm, respectively. In B-R (pH = 7) buffer solution, L-CDs had blue and red emission bands at different excitation wavelengths. As the ethanol content increased, L-CDs displayed two emission peaks when excited at 360 nm. Fixing the ethanol content at 50 %, the fluorescence intensity and UV–vis absorption emitted by L-CDs varied from pH 2.0 to 11.0. Meanwhile, the color of the solution transformed significantly under both sunlight and 365 nm UV light. L-CDs was used for pH imaging of living cells and successfully differentiated between cancer and normal cells. Furthermore, L-CDs was combined with cellulose filter paper to prepare pH test paper that could be used to monitor the freshness of meat (Scheme 1 ).
Details of materials, reagents, and instrumentation were listed in Supplementary Material.

CCDs was synthesized according to the reported methods and optimized [29]. 816 mg L-/D-Trp was added to a combination of 30 mL of ethanol and 3 mL of concentrated hydrochloric acid and stirred until completely dissolved, followed by the addition of 432 mg OPD and stirred for 20 min. The mixture was transferred to an autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 7 h. Cooled to room temperature, the resulting solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane and dialyzed with a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h. After obtaining CCDs through freeze-drying, a 20 mg/mL solution was set up and kept at 4 °C.

10 mg of L-CDs was mixed with 20 mL of absolute ethanol and stirred at 600 rpm for 30 min. Another 2 mL of TEOS was dispersed into 10 mL of absolute ethanol, which was added to the L-CDs solution, then 1 mL of ammonia (25 %) was added, and the reaction was stirred at 600 rpm for 24 h. At the end of the reaction, the product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm/min for 10 min. The supernatant was discarded and the precipitate was washed with deionized water, and dried under vacuum at 40 °C

B-R buffer solution was composed of boric acid, phosphoric acid, and acetate, the pH of the solution was adjusted by NaOH under the measurement of pH meter. In the pH determination experiments, 1 mL of absolute ethanol was added to 1 mL of B-R buffer solution of different pH and 50 μL of L-CDs (20 mg/mL) was placed in the above solution and incubated for 1 min.

Cancerous HepG2 cells and normal HL-7702 cells were used to assess the differentiating effect of L-CDs on cancerous and normal cells. HepG2 and HL-7702 cells were cultured in high glucose Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1 % antibiotics. L-CDs (200 μg/mL in DMEM medium) was incubated with the two kinds of cells for 30 min at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere of 5 % CO2, and subsequently washed three times with PBS buffer, and imaged under a laser confocal microscope, respectively. L-CDs was excited at 405 nm, blue emission was collected in the 420–470 nm range, and red emission was collected in the 600–650 nm range.

Firstly, the cellulose filter paper was cut into squares of equal size (2 cm × 2 cm) and then soaked in ethanol solution of L-CDs (20 mg/mL) for 30 min, and dried at 30 °C for 12 h. The dried test paper was soaked in B-R buffer solution with different pH for 30 min, followed by drying at 30 °C for 12 h for subsequent sensing detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring the freshness of meat samples
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh shrimp and pork were purchased from a local supermarket in Taiyuan. Sterilized Petri dishes were used to hold the shrimp and pork, and a test paper was fixed at the top of the petri dishes. Then, the meat was stored at 4 °C and 25 ℃ for different times. The color change of pH test paper was observed under 365 nm UV light during this period.

Since the only distinction between L-CDs and D-CDs was their Chiral signals, the characterizations described below were represented by L-CDs. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) picture (Fig. 1 a) demonstrated that the L-CDs was uniformly distributed and homogeneous in size with a mean diameter of 3.1 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1b) was observed that the L-CDs had typical lattice stripes of 0.21 nm, which was the same as the planar lattice spacing of graphene (100 facets). XRD was used to characterize the crystalline structure (Fig. 1c). L-CDs displayed a wide diffraction peak at 22.86°, suggesting that the (002) diffraction plane was related to the graphite structure, which revealed the amorphous structure of L-CDs. Moreover, due to the surface defects generated during the synthesis process, the diffraction peak of L-CDs shifted compared with the graphite structure's standard peak (26.5°) [30]. The functional groups on the surface of L-CDs were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). In Fig. 1d, the absorption peak at 3404 cm−1 was caused by OH/NH stretching vibration. Compared with L-Trp and OPD, the peak of L-CDs was broader and shifted toward lower wavenumber, which might be due to the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds [29]. The peaks at 2979 cm−1 and 1743 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CH and CO, respectively. The signal at 1617 cm−1 was assigned to the skeleton vibration of the benzene ring. The peaks at 1231 cm−1 and 744 cm−1 coincided ideally with the CN stretching vibration and NH deformation vibration. These data showed that the surface of L-CDs contained amino, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and other functional groups. Moreover, the spectra of L-CDs was similar to L-Trp, demonstrating the presence of L-Trp residues retained on the surface.

Subsequently, the elements and compositions of L-CDs were measured by X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS). The L-CDs consisted of three main elements: C (76.52 %), N (12.36 %), and O (11.13 %), corresponding to the three peaks of 298.3 eV (C1s), 410.3 eV (N1s), and 545.3 eV (O1s) (Fig. 2 a). The high-resolution C1s spectrum showed four peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.3 eV, 286.4 eV, and 289.0 eV, attributed to CC/CC, CN, CO, and CO, respectively (Fig. 2b, Table S1). The highest fitted peak for CC/CC was 284.6 eV, proving that the sp2 carbon was the major configuration [31]. The high-resolution N1s could be deconvoluted into three peaks of pyridine N (399.5 eV), pyrrole N (400.0 eV), and graphitic N (401.7 eV). The graphitized N content was 49.37 %, which suggested a high degree of graphitization of L-CDs (Fig. 2c, Table S1). The high-resolution O1s spectrum displayed two peaks at 531.6 eV (CO) and 533.3 eV (CO), indicating the existence of both carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of L-CDs (Fig. 2d, Table S1).

The UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of L-CDs solution were illustrated in Fig. 3 a. At 219 nm and 282 nm, two UV absorption peaks matched the π–π* and n–π* transitions, respectively. The broad absorption peak around 520 nm suggested the existence of large conjugate structures in L-CDs. The excitation spectrum of L-CDs exhibited two peaks, one was around 337 nm as the center with a narrow and symmetrical peak in the blue spectral region, and the other was in the range of 525 nm–570 nm with a wide and asymmetric peak in the red spectral region. The fluorescence emission spectrum of L-CDs was measured at different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 3b). L-CDs showed only one fluorescence emission peak in the blue spectral region when the excitation wavelength was between 330 and 380 nm, and the positions of the fluorescence emission peaks varied with the excitation wavelength. Research showed that excitation-dependent originated from various energy gap traps formed by CDs surface functional groups [32]. L-CDs displayed fluorescence emission in the red spectral region when the excitation wavelength was adjusted between 540 and 590 nm. The change of excitation wavelength only resulted in the change of fluorescence intensity, with no impact on the emission peak's position. Therefore, we speculated that there were two emission centers in L-CDs, the blue emission band was due to the surface state effect, while the red emission band attributed to the quantum confinement effect caused by the sp2 domains of L-CDs.

To explore the luminescence mechanism of L-CDs, L-CDs@SiO2 was synthesized, which forms a silica cover layer over the L-CDs. The functional groups of L-CDs@SiO2 were significantly reduced compared with L-CDs, and a strong absorption peak corresponding to Si-O-Si stretching vibration appeared at 1099 cm−1, and the peak at 461 cm−1 was correlated with SiOC stretching vibration, suggesting L-CDs was successfully encapsulated by SiO2 (Fig. S1a). In addition, the UV–vis absorption and excitation spectra of L-CDs@SiO2 were studied (Fig. S1b). Compared to L-CDs, the excitation spectrum of L-CDs@SiO2 had lower intensity at 337 nm and higher intensity at 525 nm to 570 nm. Similarly, the fluorescence emission spectra of L-CDs@SiO2 was examined at different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 3c). Unlike L-CDs, the blue emission band fluorescence strength of L-CDs@SiO2 was extremely weak and the shape of the emission peak was also greatly altered, while the red emission band was almost invariable. SiO2 was bound to L-CDs surface functional groups through covalent bonds, weakening the surface state effect of the L-CDs, which caused a reduction of the fluorescence intensity at the blue emission band, while the red emission band was unaffected [33].

In B-R (pH = 7) buffer solution, an emission peak at 438 nm was observed when L-CDs was excited at 360 nm. Nevertheless, L-CDs at the same excitation source also generated an emission peak at 608 nm with the increase of ethanol content. The fluorescence intensity increased with the solution changing from blue to purplish-red (Fig. 4 a). The value of F608/F438 increased continuously when the ethanol content was increased from 0 % to 50 %. At the same time, the growth of F608/F438 was slow after 50 % (Fig S2), so the ethanol content was fixed to 50 % in subsequent experiments. Fig. 4b demonstrated that there were two emission peaks when the excitation wavelength ranged from 320 nm to 420 nm, while the optimized excitation wavelength at 360 nm. The TEM images demonstrated that the L-CDs aggregated into larger particles when the ethanol content was increased to 50 % (Fig. 4c). Meanwhile, the DLS plots revealed the mean diameter of L-CDs increased to 270.22 nm (Fig. S3). The results indicated that the increase in ethanol content triggered the AIE effect. The absorption peak of L-CDs at 520 nm partially overlapped with the blue emission band. In addition, The fluorescence lifetime of L-CDs at 438 nm decreased from 9.09 ns to 8.58 ns with the addition of ethanol, demonstrating that Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) might occur (Fig S4, Table S2) [34]. Thus, the dual-emission mechanism of L-CDs was revealed in Scheme 2 . The blue emission originated from the surface state, while the red emission was due to the sp2 domains of L-CDs. The AIE effect was triggered by an increase in ethanol content, which led to a decrease in the distance between CDs. When the distance was less than R0 (Förster distance, 1–10 nm), L-CDs with large sp2 domain conjugated structures acted as FRET receptors and the emission in the blue spectral region was reabsorbed to produce a red emission band [35]. The combined effects of AIE and FRET resulted in a dual-emission effect of L-CDs, with emission peaks at 438 nm and 608 nm at 360 nm excitation wavelength.

The chirality of the precursors L-/D-Trp, OPD, and their products L-/D-CDs were analyzed by circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy. L-/D-Trp generated a symmetric CD signal near 227 nm, while OPD showed no CD signal (Fig. S5). L-/D-CDs inherited the CD signal of chiral source near 227 nm, which might be a result of the synthesis temperature of the CCDs (160℃) being much lower than the melting point of L-/D-Trp (282℃) so that the chiral structure of the raw material can be preserved in the L-CDs. Furthermore, the L-/D-CDs also generated a new CD signal at 255–300 nm that could be attributable to the π-π* leaps of the aromatic domains in the chiral environment (Fig. 4d) [36].

The effect of precursor ratios, reaction time, and reaction temperature of CDs were researched. Fluorescence intensity reached the highest when the concentration ratio of OPD to L-Trp was 1:1(Fig.S6). The continued addition of L-Trp reduced the fluorescence intensity due to the self-polymerization of L-CDs. The CD signal increased with the content of chiral precursors. When the reaction temperature was 160 °C, L-CDs had the best dual-emission performance (Fig S7). Further increasing the reaction temperature, excessive carbonization of the precursor decomposed the carbon core, leading to a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the red emission band. As the reaction temperature increased, the chiral structure of the precursor was disrupted and the CD signal decreased continuously. Then the effect of reaction time was investigated (Fig S8). The fluorescence intensity was the maximum after 7 h of reaction time. The CD signals were almost constant with the reaction time extended from 5 h to 9 h. Therefore, the optimal conditions for synthesizing L-CDs for subsequent experiments were determined as a reactant ratio of 1:1 and a reaction temperature of 160 °C for 7 h.

Quinine sulfate (0.01 M H2SO4, QY = 0.56) and rhodamine B (ethanol, QY = 0.89) were used as the standard to investigate the relative quantum yield of L-CDs, which were 23 % (blue spectral region) and 22 % (red spectral region), respectively. The photobleaching resistance, long-term storage stability, and thermal stability were measured (Fig. S9). The optical properties of the L-CDs solution remained almost unchanged after continuous irradiation under a 365 nm UV lamp for 60 min, indicating that the L-CDs were highly resistant to photobleaching. The luminescence of L-CDs solution had no remarkable change following a 40-day storage period at 4 ℃. The fluorescence emission exhibited consistent stability with the ambient temperature was changed from 10 °C to 60 °C. These findings suggested that L-CDs possessed robust fluorescence characteristics that endured diverse environmental conditions, thus enhancing the potential utility of L-CDs in sensing applications.

The optical characteristics of L-CDs were tested in a mixed system of ethanol and B-R buffer solution with different pH (v/v, 1/1). The fluorescence intensity of L-CDs responded to pH ranging from 2.0 to 11.0, and the solution could be observed to shift from navy blue to purplish-red, and finally to pale pink. The fluorescence intensity at 438 nm continuously decreased from pH 2.0 to 11.0. At 608 nm, the emission peaks appeared progressively and the fluorescence intensity gradually enhanced from pH 2.0 to 8.5, and the fluorescence intensity at 608 nm weakened from pH 8.5 to 11.0 (Fig. 5 a and b). F608/F438 was correlated linearly with pH between 4.0 and 8.0 (Fig. 5c). Correspondingly, the absorption spectra of L-CDs responded in the pH range from 2.0 to 11.0 of the B-R solution, and the solution color changed from blue through purple to dark pink(Fig. 5d and e). A540/A630 was correlated linearly with pH between 5.0 and 9.0 (Fig. 5f).

It was revealed that the response of CDs to pH originated from the protonation and deprotonation of surface functional groups [37]. Under acidic conditions, the functional groups were protonated. When the pH was increased, the surface functional groups were gradually deprotonated, resulting in a decreased fluorescence intensity at 438 nm. At the same time, the L-CDs surface functional groups and the conjugate structure of carbon nuclear underwent an intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) effect, leading to a new peak at 608 nm [38]. The zeta potential values of L-CDs ranged from +2.57 to −3.99 mV with increasing pH of the B-R solution (Fig. S10), suggesting that the protonation/deprotonation process occurred in response to changing pH. The change in pH did not significantly alter the absorption spectra of the L-CDs, indicating that a ground state complex did not form thus static quenching had not occurred (Fig. S11a). The fluorescence lifetime of L-CDs at 438 nm decreased from 12.10 ns to 7.90 ns by decreasing H+ concentration (Fig. S11b, Table S3). Then, L-CDs in different pH solutions of temperature change experiments were performed, and it was found that as the temperature rose, the Stern-Volmer constant of the system increased (Fig. S11c). Therefore, it could be speculated that fluorescence quenching at 438 nm was caused by the dynamic quenching process [39].

Considering the reversibility of pH changed in the biological environment, the optical characteristics of L-CDs were detected by cycling the pH value between 4.0 and 8.0. The proportional fluorescence intensities F608/F438 of L-CDs displayed good reproducibility between pH 4.0 and 8.0 (Fig. 6 a). The photostability of L-CDs at different pH was examined, and Fig. 6b showed that L-CDs demonstrated excellent fluorescence stability under different pH values. The effect of ionic strength (expressed as NaCl concentration) on the fluorescence property was evaluated. Fig. 6c displayed that the value of F608/F438 was almost constant in the existence of different concentrations of NaCl at pH 4.0 and 8.0, which favored the application of L-CDs under practical conditions. For pH imaging of living cells, potential interference from physiologically essential metal ions and some small biological molecules had to be excluded. The anti-interference properties of L-CDs were determined at pH 4.0 and 8.0, as shown in Fig. 6d, high concentrations of metal ions and amino acid small molecules had almost no effect on the value of F608/F438 at different pH values. The above results suggested that L-CDs had good reversibility, photostability, and anti-interference in the pH 4.0–8.0, which could apply to cellular imaging and accurate measurement of pH in cellular environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Distinguishing normal cells and cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that the pH of the tumor cell environment is slightly lower than that of normal cells. Compared with the pH value of the physiological micro-environment (7.2–7.4), the pH value of the tumor microenvironment (6.2–6.9) is slightly lower [40]. Therefore, whether L-CDs could precisely distinguish cancer cells and normal cells by its specific response to different pH was further explored. To investigate the performance of L-CDs in biological applications, the toxic effect of L-CDs was determined by CCK-8 assay (Fig. S12). It could be seen intuitively that the cell viability still remained >85 % after normal cells HL-7702 and cancer cells HepG2 were incubated with different concentrations of L-CDs (0–400 μg/mL, 24 h), illuminating L-CDs presented low cytotoxicity. The imaging performance of L-CDs in HepG2 cells and HL-7702 cells were compared. It demonstrated that HepG2 cells displayed stronger blue fluorescence. On the contrary, there was a higher level of red fluorescence in HL-7702 cells(Fig. 7 ). Quantitative analysis of the fluorescence images revealed that HepG2 cells exhibited a considerably lower fluorescence intensity ratio (FRed/FBlue) than HL-7702 cells, which was in line with the spectral data. Both blue and red channels displayed characteristic pH-dependent signals, indicating that L-CDs had excellent cell penetration and were able to differentiate between normal and cancer cells using a ratiometric method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper-based sensor for pH detection in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: Paper-based sensors had the advantage of in situ, real-time, and visual detection. pH test papers based on L-CDs was prepared for pH monitoring of real samples (shrimp and pork) (Fig. 8 a). In Fig. 8b, the color of test paper shifted from light blue through light purple to red with increasing darkness as the pH increased under sunlight. Similarly, the color of the test paper gradually changed from blue to purple-red and finally to dark red under UV light. The above findings indicated that the L-CDs was able to detect pH by visualization.

The freshness of shrimp and pork was assessed using prepared pH test paper (Fig. 8c). The color of the paper did not change visibly when the shrimp were stored at 4 °C for 48 h, demonstrating the freshness of shrimp remained by this storage condition. After 24 h of storage at 25 °C, the color of the test paper shifted from blue to purplish-red and started turn to red under UV light. After 48 h, the shrimp completely deteriorated and the test paper showed deep red fluorescence. There was only a small color change in the test paper of pork samples after 48 h of storage at 25 °C. The pork deteriorated after 120 h, while the test paper showed red fluorescence. This difference could be attributed to the higher protein content of the shrimp, causing it to spoil faster and release more BAs. The above results suggested that test strips based on L-CDs could be used for visual monitoring of meat freshness.

In summary, CCDs with dual-emission was synthesized for the first time. Two emission centers existed in the prepared L-CDs. With the increase of ethanol content, the distance between CDs was decreased and the FRET phenomenon occurred so that the L-CDs demonstrated two emission peaks at 360 nm excitation wavelength. CD spectra displayed that L-CDs generated chiral signals at both 200–255 nm and 255–300 nm. Moreover, L-CDs had excellent resistance to photobleaching, and temperature stability, and their fluorescence property remained unchanged under long-term storage, thus it had good application prospects in the field of sensing. More significantly, L-CDs, known for their sensitivity as a fluorescent probe, could respond to pH in solution and living cells and successfully differentiate between HepG2 cells and HL-7702 cells, which provided an identification method for the early diagnosis of cancer cells. pH test paper based on L-CDs was prepared to monitor pH changes in shrimp and pork from freshness to spoilage. The above results indicated that the construction of a pH visual sensing platform based on synthetic L-CDs had many potential applications in food freshness monitoring and cellular imaging.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125316.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
